item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an idea for a scip. It's not fleshed out and really does feel like it's unfinished. I also vehemently disagree with the idea that the entries brevity is a strength. A test log or better yet an interview with one of the couples who received one of these would add a whole extra layer of both sadness and creep. As it stands a firm -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is this "Safe", given that while the robots are contained safely, the Foundation can't be sure that they've gotten all of them, as well as the fact that, and this is important, there is an unknown entity or organization creating and distributing these items, of which the Foundation has absolutely no information on.
Sure, each individual instance is contained safely (well, except for whatever happened in Incident 1298-a, whatever that was), but whatever's making and distributing them sure isn't. It depends on a Taskforce hearing about a couple having lost a kid and having a doll turn up on their front door. Unless they hear about each and every instance of infant mortality, there's bound to be a few slip through the cracks.

This just seems to be at least Euclid, and definitely more work put into "who the hell is making these?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Im really sorry but this just doesnt do anything for me. Theres no hook, nothing other than wierd robot babies being delivered to your door, and thats it. I know this is pretty old but I dont get anything from it.
I can see a germ of a good idea maybe but as it stands -1, if it gets a rewrite Iĺl reconsider

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing the 'unfinished/not quite enough' feeling. Early in the comments, Author, you were saying multiple times that you didn't have any direction to go in but to, say, make it more dangerous, but that's patently false.

You mention Incident 1298-a which affects the containment procedures, but never expand on the incident. That's one great source of potential to give this the little kick it needs to be truly outstanding.

If you don't want to go into that, which I feel is the best route, you can also focus on the 'human' element that you claim to want, and have a recovery log or interview with one of the people who got one of these babies - to drive home WHY, say, one of them would fight to keep an obviously fake baby, and get the feels rolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One or several images in this article have been removed due to non-compliance with the Creative Commons license. They may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact apoplexic or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.


This automated message was sent on behalf of apoplexic. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact apoplexic in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending is abrupt and it feels unfinished. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Four years later, I've figured out what this needs! A sample of the notes left with the babies!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like others have said: feels unfinished. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't honestly upvote this. It feels incomplete. There's a mention of a fairly serious sounding incident, but no incident log. There's a mention of one specific instance (1298-5) being disassembled and put in cold storage until a certain point, but nothing that tells us why that is.

Like I said, it feels incomplete. It's like you baited the hook, but never cast the line. You're just… sitting on the boat with a dying worm on a string… taunting the fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels boring, and I think you ought to have these be Euclid class at least. They aren't fully contained so long as something is distributing them in the middle of the night. Also, the entity sending them out ought to be 1298-1, since it's part of the anomoly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe a school bus crashed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got to say that this is a good concept, however you mention an Incident 1298-A that stopped all long term tests, but never say what happened or what tests have already been performed. It seems unfinished as we apparently have done something with them, and at one point they were a threat, but we never get to know why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wait, this doesn't make sense. who gets put on the news for losing their firstborn child? that can't be a thing, can it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's boring, give it a story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is missing that extra oomph in my opinion. Added with the second paragraph of the Containment Procedures, I have to agree with Drewbear about how this feels unfinished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah man, this doesn't fly by me.

Disassembly has shown no electronic components, power sources, or complex machinery, though active instances maintain a temperature roughly analogous to human norms.

So we're ignoring the fact that the image has a USB plug or something for a hand? Also, this article seems to like using the word "though" a little more than I think it should.

So we can't find anything electronic inside, nothing powering it, and nothing considered "complex" inside, yet the biggest mystery is how it maintains a human body temperature? Not how does it move then? Not how does it react to stimuli?

Though instances will consume milk or soft foods, they do not produce waste and anything ingested will remain inside the predominantly hollow interior.

This raises the question of what happens to that stuff put inside it then? Does it eventually fill up and stop ingesting? Does it expand to fit more food stuffs? And if it all just sits in there, doesn't it start smelling horrible? Anyone ever smelt milk gone bad? What about milk that has been left out at above room temperature? Say, the average temperature of the human body?

A few holes in this that I don't think should fly. Feels like this is a decent start to something but it needs expanding upon. Should not be allowed to fly because, "robo babies are creep." -1

Edit: Also, comment by the author says something about these things, "growing up" but I'm not getting anything like that from the article. Question mark?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Boring as hell. Robot baby someone threw away. Whoopty fucking doo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote. -1 This feels right on the brink of being interesting.

I like brief and simple SCPs, but I don't think the brevity is your greatest strength here, as you say— I think the strength is the potential in the human element, the implied reactions and interactions. You don't give us quite enough to imagine/imply based on the scip's qualities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HOW IS BABBY FORMED?

Well if you'll step over here, you'll see sprocket A goes into slot C.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe have it become more human over time, whilst growing?

You could also add that, as it grows, its 'personality' changes?

Otherwise, very good. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot as-is. It's weird, it has a story to tell, and it tells it. Simple and good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little addition to see if it gives a little bit more of a hook. The obvious interpretation is that SCP-1298-5 is "growing up", but exactly what it's growing into is left up in the air. Feedback welcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I might have something.

The thing with the notes is that their contents are immaterial- the point of the line is to indicate something about the creators (namely, their complete lack of actual understanding of human beings) as opposed to information about the instances. Added complexity, in my opinion, removes the effectiveness of what is present. I want to try something different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but it does seem to be missing something. There's the description of the appearance of the notes, but no samples — that's an obvious place to build on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should throw in some more. Maybe the letter, maybe a recovery log…? I ain't an expert. Maybe give the dolls something awful that they do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God help me, I cracked up at 'MTF Omega-8 "Babysnatchers".

"Babysnatcher 1, this is Babysnatcher 4, come in, over."

Seriously, though, you've pegged tone perfectly. The article itself doesn't do a lot for me - the SCP seems like a 'doll what works through magic', and I genuinely can't see how any but the most grief-deranged individuals could fail to give these things up. If we hadn't dissected one to see how they were put together, I'd assume this thing was just a weirdly-advanced doll of completely ordinary manufacture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely has potential, though in its present form I would agree that it seems to lack a hook. My initial reading was that instances of 1298 were the parents' lost child, returned in a transformed state (or else something like the changeling child folk tale). I want to like this as it has a very spartan quality that seems to hearken back to entries like 172, but at the minute it just feels incomplete for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Solidly meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most subjects have been upper-middle class Caucasian couples located in the Pacific Northwest of the United States who have recently lost a child, usually their first.

My heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh wow.
And their purpose is merely attempting to observe humans in their natural habitat to better emulate them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1377 was on my mind while I was writing this. One of my favourite SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to imagine SCP-1377 specimens delivering these little guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Leave it be, it's good.
Hell, Bright has upvoted, and that's goddamn rare.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are only three places I can see to take this. Either these babies grow up, which will add way too much length to the article, they do something sinister, which undermines the "trying to help" concept, or they have a compulsive effect, which is silly and undermines the human psychology play.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good start, but I feel like it's missing something. It needs to go one step further, though I don't know what that step could be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was pretty much the target I was aiming for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not going to vote, yet. On one hand, I find it charming, and on the other hand, incredibly unnerving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Doorstep Babies. I wanted to wait until I knew it would be sticking around before I added it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't think of any way to extend this without compromising it's brevity, which I feel is its strongest element, or just adding "kills you/crazies you to death." I feel the concept works best if it is just a poorly rendered imitation of a baby, and any effects such as treating them as our own is caused by grief and other standard human psychology. It's still anamalous and it still needs to be contained.

Also, the component sticking out isn't that complex really. The point of the sentence is simply to relate that these are not clockwork or otherwise somewhat mundane in construction. They are machines, but not of a pattern the Foundation considers normal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Like VAElynx said, it's a number between 30 and 39: Redacting it is pointless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except this and the Tower of Babble are nothing alike.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but you left out a very vital piece of information in the containment procedure when you blanked that number. Pretend your a gaurd watching this cold storage unit and there's a power surge (let's say 334 is having an off day) and the babies get out. It's not a huge issue, they're babies, pick them up and put them all back.

Except, no one told you how many you had to start with. You might need to catch 30 or it could be 39. Don't worry, it's not like letting adorable little skips wander the halls has ever bit the Foundation in the ass before...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that it's a solid concept, but lacks a hook. There are a few different things that you could focus on in this piece. I'd avoid the 'alien trying to help' angle; Tower of Babble has that pretty much sewn up. But it seems like you've got something interesting lurking behind this article. You just need to let a little more of it come through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this have a title?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find them to be both. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure whether I should find these things kind of cute or terrifyingly creepy… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to point some things out.
I think these are errors

Instances of SCP-1298 delivered by unknown means

I think that needs an 'are' in there.

A statically significant number of subjects

I think you wanted 'statistically' here.

I'm not sure whether or not these are intentional, and I'm hesitant to edit this.

I upvoted. I think this is an interesting idea and I don't want to see it deleted, but I think it could definitely be executed better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Disassembly has shown no electronic components, power sources, or complex machinery

Aaaand then the photo has the robo-baby with some sort of electronic/robotic component where its hand should be.

Also, this feel… unfinished. I don't think that you need to make it dangerous or scary, as creepy and sad definitely works with this concept, but it really does feel like there's a paragraph or two missing from the end.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sorta like the concept of replacement babies and everything…

…But this goes nowhere. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself wondering what delivers these to people's houses. The whole concept creeps me out and the tone is wonderful, so have an upvote!

Edit: However, I do agree that it would be prudent to replace the 3█ with an actual number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Simple and safe, with just enough detail to keep me interested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Replace the absurd 3█ with an actual number, and I'll upvote.
And done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered that strongly before settling on the idea that it would not grow because that is not in the motives of the entity that creates them, who probably does not understand that children become adults, and because the implied imagery of a desperate family hanging onto one for far longer than natural was more powerful to me. I also wanted to keep the article brief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This could have been infinitely creepy, but instead it's just kind of strange.
Imagine if these things were 'programmed' to progress at the same pace as a human (with or without parental input). They'd learn to crawl, to walk, to speak basic words, and maybe even string together disturbing, chaotic sentences. Could have even interviewed one that was a couple of years old.

And yet, leaning toward downvote.

EDIT:
I want to add that the tone is perfect and that you're improving :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We have storks already, but we don't have, to my knowledge, the classic doorstep baby basket trope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No storks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mention of the note is what did it for me. If it was simply evil baby robots that some evil corporation made, it would be boring, but the idea that there's something out there that is able to understand the basest rudiments of human culture but is completely unable to actually understand what anything means is amazing.

Basically, it's like an SCP version of the Toynbee tiles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The point is the old doorstep baby trope gone horribly weird. Not everything has to murder/drive everyone crazy.

My thought process was roughly that this is what the well-meaning version of SCP-1377's creator(s) does. Whatever it is that makes these things, it doesn't really "get" humans, as displayed by the nonsensical letters, and is trying to cheer up those sad people on the TV. By delivering a little chunk of the uncanny valley straight to their doorstep.

Also, imagine what recovery must be like in instances where the distraught couple have taken the little buggers in. Anything you put in just remains inside, sloshing around, heated by the "body heat", going stale. And yet they'll fight the Foundation's agents over them, not due to an anomalous effect but just human nature.
